text: At this point, the sac covering the heart has been opened.
First, the connections to the heart-lung machine are placed in the aorta and then in the heart itself.
The patient is placed on the heart-lung machine,
A clamp is placed across the aorta separating the heart from the heart-lung machine.
Then the heart is temporarily stopped with a special cold preservative solution called cardioplegia.
The aorta is opened, blood removed, and the diseased aortic valve is seen.
The valve is a three-leaflet valve with extensive calcification that limits the opening of the valve, thus creating aortic stenosis.
The valve leaflets are carefully removed.
The calcium which was left behind in growing into the aortic wall is removed to allow secure valve placement.
Afterwards, a sizing device is placed to where the valve once was to allow determination of the correct size of the replacement valve.
Sutures are then placed around the insertion point of the valve.
Once placed, these will be used to secure the new valve in place.
The aortic valve replacement is brought onto the surgical table.
In this case, it is a bovine pericardial bioprosthesis.
It is on a holder for better handling.
The sutures are then placed to the bottom of the replacement valve.
Using the holder, the valve is lowered into the aorta and set at the level of the patient's original valve.
The holder is now removed.
The sutures are tied and cut.
The valve is now seen at the junction between the patient's heart and the aorta.
It is inspected and seen to be fine.
The incision in the aorta is closed.
After completion of the closure of the aorta, the clamp is removed and the heart restarted.
Finally, the heart without the heart-lung machine connections is inspected for bleeding.
This will be followed by closure of the small incision.
The patient did well, receiving no blood transfusions.
He was off the ventilator just a few hours after surgery.
This was followed by an uneventful recovery and discharge from the hospital less than a week later.
